insights: Low and Medium priority Cost Reduction goals achieve 87.27% and 91.49% success rates respectively—more than double the overall baseline of 37.82%—driven primarily by realistic target percentages (mean 70.51% for successful vs 95.09% for unsuccessful goals within this group).

Across all goal categories, success rate declines monotonically as target_percentage increases, with ≤65% targets yielding optimal success universally—yet only 32–37% of goals in each category currently adopt this range.

Cost Reduction goals outperform all other categories by 39–66 percentage points at every comparable target level (e.g., 97.6% vs 41.6% at ≤65% targets), indicating structural advantages—such as metric measurability or departmental execution capability—that amplify the impact of achievable targets.

High and Critical priority Cost Reduction goals show near-total failure (0% and 40% success) despite only marginally higher mean target percentages (78.33% vs 73.16% for Low/Medium), confirming that priority-driven target inflation—not inherent difficulty—is the dominant cause of low success.

Marketing department achieves 100% success on Low/Medium priority Cost Reduction goals (19/19), the highest among all departments, suggesting localized best practices in target calibration and execution discipline that are transferable across categories.

Unsuccessful Low/Medium priority Cost Reduction goals take significantly longer to close (228.4 days mean) than successful ones (198.7 days), indicating that overly ambitious targets—even at low priority—induce operational drag and reduce goal velocity.
summary: The unexpectedly high success rates of Low and Medium priority Cost Reduction goals are not anomalies but the result of disciplined, realistic target setting—primarily within the ≤65% range—combined with category-specific advantages in measurability and execution. This target-success relationship is universal across all goal categories, yet only one-third of current goals leverage the optimal ≤65% target band. Cost Reduction’s consistent 40–66 percentage point advantage over other categories at identical target levels signals that its structural strengths (e.g., clear metrics, Marketing department leadership) make it a high-fidelity testbed for scalable goal management practices. To enhance effectiveness across all categories, organizations should systematically recalibrate targets into the ≤65% range and institutionalize cross-departmental sharing of target-setting discipline—starting with Marketing’s proven approach—while treating priority level as a signal for target conservatism, not ambition.
